details.text: Derivative: مُنْفَكِّينَ is from اِنْفَكَّ – اِنْفِكَاك which means to separate and break up. It can also imply to discontinue or depart from a certain course or practices i.e., to stop doing something. اِنْفِكَاك is upon the اِنْفِعَال (Infi’aal) pattern form which indicates that the action happens to itself. As such مُنْفَكِّينَ can mean to separate from each other i.e., into different groups of believers and disbelievers.

Derivative: بَيِّنَةُ (Bayyinah) comes from بَانَ – يَبِينُ which means to put distance between two things so that one becomes clear from another. بَيِّنَةُ (Bayyinah) has been explained to refer to: The clear proof or evidence that separates and distinguishes between the truth and falsehood.

لَمْ يَكُنِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ وَالْمُشْرِكِينَ مُنْفَكِّينَ حَتَّىٰ تَأْتِيَهُمُ الْبَيِّنَةُ

- The understanding of مِنْ “From” here in the statement: لَمْ يَكُنِ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ أَهْلِ الْكِتَابِ وَالْمُشْرِكِينَ can change the understanding of the verse. مِنْ “From” can be تَبْعِيضِيَة (Tab’eediyah) which means it refers to some from the whole i.e., some of the People of the Book and some of the Idolaters. مِنْ “From” can also be بَيانِيَة (Bayaaniyah) which means it is explanatory and elaborative in which case it would include and encompass all of the People of the Book and Idolaters. The first implies something “from” something and the second meaning implies “in terms of”. The verse would therefore read: “Those who disbelieve from among the people of the book and the idolators” or “Those who disbelieve in terms of the people of the book and the idolators”.
Derivative: يَتْلُو is from تَلا – تِلَاوَةً which means to read and to follow on. Reading is when the reader follows the words and lines on a page.

رَسُولٌ مِنَ اللَّهِ يَتْلُو صُحُفًا مُطَهَّرَةً

- “A Messenger from Allah reciting purified scriptures or pages” – This is the explanation of بَيِّنَةُ (Bayyinah) mentioned in the previous verse. When you have a Messenger and a revelation or message recited by that Messenger you have بَيِّنَةُ (Bayyinah) i.e., a clear proof.

Derivative: كُتُبٌ is the plural of كَتِاب (Kitaab) which means book but is also used in Arabic to refer to law. The root of كُتُبٌ is كَتَبَ which means to write but also to mandate and make compulsory i.e., to set as a law. كُتُبٌ (Kutub) here is therefore synonymous with the word أَحْكَام (Ahkaam – Rulings or Laws).
Derivative: قَيِّمَةٌ refers to something that stands upright and straight and is free of crookedness.

- “Those who (الَّذِينَ) were given (أُوتُوا) the Book (الْكِتَابَ)”. Whenever the passive voice is used in the Qur’an in reference to those who had been given the Book, with the passive verb form أُوتُوا “were given”, the speech following it is negative and critical. When the active voice is used آتَيْنَا “We gave” the statement that follows it is positive, for example verse 121 of Surah Al-Baqarah.

Derivative: الْقَيِّمَةِ can mean firmly established and it has also been said to be attached to an omitted but understood word which is مِلَّة (Millah - Nation) to give the meaning of established or upright nation. الْقَيِّمَةِ refers to that which is of the highest level of اِسْتِقامَة (Istiqaamah – Uprightness).

Derivative: حُنَفَاءَ – الحَنَفُ (Al-Hanaf) is the inclination away from misguidance or falsehood to the true Deen and uprightness. It can carry the implication of devotion and not be distracted. حَنِيف (Haneef) the singular of حُنَفَاءَ was attributed to Ibrahim (as).

Derivative: يَعْبُدُوا is from عَبَدَ – يَعْبُدُ - عِبَادَة includes the meaning of worship but also has another distinctly different connotation and that is of slavery and servitude. Ibn Taymiyah (rh) states five components of slavery to Allah: Love for Allah that is greater than love for anything else, obedience to Allah in everything, sincerity to Allah in making everything for Allah’s sake, absolute trust and reliance (Tawakkul) in Allah, and finally the terms of slavery which are dictated by the Master (الرَّبّ).
- “They make the Deen sincerely for His sake”. the لَهُ (for him) has ben advanced in the sentence structure which implies exclusivity i.e., it is only for him alone that they make the Deen sincere.
Derivative: الْبَرِيَّةِ comes from بَرَأَ which means to create. الْبَرِيَّةِ refers to what was created and exists i.e., creatures or creations.
Derivative: خَالِدِينَ is from خُلْد (Khuld) which means to remain permanently somewhere.
Derivative: جَهَنَّم (Jahannam) is the name for hellfire and it has Persian origins from the Persian word Jahnam which refers to a “torture chamber”.
Derivative: إِنَّ is used for the purpose of تَوْكِيد (Tawkeed – Emphasis and reiteration). It is used to provide certainty and to confirm a fact.
Derivative: رَضِيَ – رِضاء means to be pleased to the point of complete satisfaction and contentment i.e., to the point of no complaints.
